petitioner_background: The petitioner is a female model from Great Britain seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. She worked as a Page 3 glamour pinup model for the British tabloid The Sun, appeared in print advertisements for companies including ICI, Interflora, Black and White, Adidas, and St. Troy Lycra Sensations by Dupont, and modeled the 1998 spring collection for design company JonValdi. She authored a book titled 'Storm in a D Cup' (a series of interviews with 12 Page 3 girls) and wrote several columns for The Sun. After the filing date, she also worked as a model for Clear Choice Satellite, served as managing editor for the Santa Monica Observer, and was involved in projects with PrairieFire Productions and MGM.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted two brief articles about the publication of her book 'Storm in a D Cup'; one brief article commenting on Princess Diana's statements about bulimia; one brief article about the petitioner's preference in dresses (copies did not reflect the publications or dates); evidence of her appearance as a pinup glamour model in the British tabloid The Sun as one of the Page 3 Girls; a story in The Sun about the petitioner's broken engagement; an unidentified newspaper story about her recovery from a dog bite six years previously; a letter from Garry Bushell, Show Business Editor for The Sun, asserting the petitioner appeared on popular British morning shows to promote her book; and two articles about her personal life published in Hello magazine after the date of filing.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that glamour shots and advertisements are not published materials about the petitioner but are the result of being able to work in her field. The AAO acknowledged that modeling is not a field where published critical analysis can be expected, and that coverage of even renowned models is generally limited to their personal lives, so frivolous tabloid coverage could count. However, the articles in Hello came after the filing date and could not establish eligibility per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. While not contesting The Sun's national circulation, the AAO found that limited coverage in the very publication publishing her glamour shots suggested her recognition was limited to fans of Page 3 pinup photographs, which is not indicative of sustained national or international acclaim. The AAO found television appearances could be comparable evidence, but Mr. Bushell's statement about such appearances was not supported by the television networks, and going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient per Matter of Treasure Craft of California.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted the petitioner contributed enormously to the modeling profession by dispelling myths that models are 'women who can only rely on their good looks and bodies to earn their living.' Mr. Bushell attested that the petitioner was ranked 'one of the top ten beauties in Great Britain' on several occasions. Evidence was submitted that petitioner wrote columns for The Sun and authored a book ('Storm in a D Cup'). On appeal, counsel asserted she was the first model to write for a women's section on topics such as health, beauty and fashion, and that her book dispelled myths about Page 3 girls.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the director that the petitioner failed to demonstrate her impact on the field of modeling. Mr. Bushell's assertions about being voted one of the top beauties were unsupported by evidence, and counsel failed to explain how such votes, even if they occurred, dispelled myths that models rely on their looks. The record lacked evidence that her authorship of a few articles and a book prior to the filing date led to the hiring of more models as writers. While she may have gone on a promotional tour, the record lacked evidence of any influence her book had on the modeling field. The AAO emphasized that not every book by an insider to the field constitutes a contribution of major significance to that field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Three articles in The Sun credited to the petitioner: 'Dial Your Dream Bachelor Boy' profiling 'the tastiest top ten fellas' in Great Britain; 'Hunks Who Give Our Page 3 Girls a Thrill' describing a photo shoot of John Huntley and the petitioner's preferences in men; and 'You Can't Bake It, Fake It' reporting her opinion on various brands of tanning make-up. A report credited to another journalist about the petitioner's method for relieving back pain. Her book 'Storm in a D Cup,' a series of interviews with 12 Page 3 girls. Counsel also referenced articles about a book tour and appearances on shows such as Good Morning Britain, and referenced articles written for the Santa Monica Observer after the date of filing.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO affirmed the director's conclusion that the articles did not provide a scholarly analysis of the art of modeling but at best constituted personal reflections on men and tanning creams, and characterizing them as addressing health and fashion issues was inaccurate. The AAO acknowledged this criterion does not apply to the field of modeling and considered whether her writing could be comparable evidence. It found her articles in The Sun not only lacked scholarly content but also did not focus on the field of modeling, so they were not persuasive. Santa Monica Observer articles post-dating filing could not establish eligibility per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. Counsel's assertion that the book was a British best seller was unsupported by any submitted evidence (none of the articles mentioned it as a best seller), and assertions of counsel do not constitute evidence per Matter of Obaigbena and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez. Promotional touring does not guarantee strong sales.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Considerable evidence of the petitioner's work as a Page 3 girl and print advertising model, and evidence of her modeling the fashion designs of JonValdi in fashion shows.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO disagreed that fashion shows are designed to exhibit the work of the models; rather, their purpose is to exhibit the work of the fashion designers, and selection is based on the designers' talents, not the models. Fashion shows thus cannot be considered artistic showcases or exhibitions of fashion models. Further, appearing in a fashion show is merely evidence that a model can secure employment, which even in a competitive field is not indicative of national or international acclaim. Glamour shots and advertisements are not artistic exhibitions or showcases. The AAO ultimately found this criterion relates to visual artists, not modeling, and the evidence was not comparable to the type of exclusive artistic exhibition or showcase contemplated by the regulations.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A letter from the President of JonValdi (a design company purportedly catering to a major celebrity clientele) asserting the petitioner modeled their 1998 spring collection. Evidence of appearances on several covers of Puzzler, other puzzle publications, and Practical Photography. Print advertisements for ICI, Interflora, Black and White, Adidas, St. Troy Lycra Sensations by Dupont, and others. After the filing date: work as a model for Clear Choice Satellite; representation of The Sun on a trip to Hamburg, Germany 'with many other celebrities' per Mr. Bushell; appearances in television commercials (per Mr. Bushell and another reference); a letter from Stephen Herzberg, Executive Producer at PrairieFire Productions, asserting the petitioner was essential to a reality-based soap opera being developed called 'Strippers'; service as managing editor for the Santa Monica Observer; and letters from individuals at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer Studios (MGM) asserting she was working on MGM's 'EDAM' project (none of these MGM letters provided her job title or description of duties).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the director that the petitioner failed to demonstrate the leading or critical nature of her roles. While acknowledging that advertising is important and the modeling industry is competitive, the AAO concluded that not every model appearing prominently in a print advertisement played a critical role for the company featured. The petitioner did not submit evidence correlating sales increases with her appearance in company advertisements. Moreover, the AAO was not persuaded that a petitioner can demonstrate a leading or critical role for a company for which she was never employed even as a contractor. She did not claim to have played a critical role for a distinguished advertising agency or studio, and the record lacked letters from studio officials claiming their success increased as a result of their use of the petitioner or that campaigns without her did not perform as well. The record also contained no evidence that the 'Strippers' show enjoyed a distinguished reputation as of or after the filing date.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel's assertion that the petitioner's commercial success was demonstrated by her appearance in advertisements for many leading companies, and the argument on appeal that her promotion and advertisement for 'such a plethora of varying products shows her tremendous commercial value for designers', manufacturers' and stores' products.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(x) makes very clear the type of evidence necessary: box office receipts or sales of media featuring the petitioner's performances. The petitioner's 'commercial value' to marketers is not comparable to the type of data evidencing commercial success specifically mandated by this criterion. The AAO noted that while a petitioner need not meet any particular criterion, the high remuneration criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ix) is more relevant to the petitioner's field of modeling than commercial success in the performing arts, yet the petitioner did not claim to meet that criterion. The director had also concluded petitioner was not a 'performing artist' and had not demonstrated that the organizations she represented enjoyed commercial success largely due to her efforts.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished herself as a model to such an extent that she could be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of her field. While the evidence indicated the petitioner showed talent as a model, it was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field. Even if the AAO were to accept Mr. Bushell's unsupported statements about television appearances to meet the published materials criterion, the evidence fell far short of meeting two other criteria. The petitioner therefore failed to establish eligibility pursuant to section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, and she did not sustain the burden of proof that remains entirely with the petitioner under section 291 of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Reg. Comm. 1971)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: No fraud findings. Procedural issues noted: several pieces of evidence (articles in Hello magazine, Santa Monica Observer writings, post-filing modeling work) post-dated the filing date and could not establish eligibility under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. Mr. Bushell's assertions about television appearances and the petitioner being voted among the top beauties were unsupported by corroborating documentary evidence, which is insufficient per Matter of Treasure Craft of California. Counsel's unsupported assertions (e.g., that the book was a British best seller) were rejected as not constituting evidence per Matter of Obaigbena and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez. Submitted article copies did not reflect the publications in which they appeared or their dates.
